reasoning: Article used before nouns repeatedly ("the whole", "the world").
Mostly links nouns in phrases like "of a whole" and "of some".
Often precedes verbs in infinitival clauses (e.g., "to be", "to show"), though there are some prepositional uses in the contexts.
Occurs as copular auxiliary ("is good", "is true").
Introduces subordinate clauses in many contexts ("that is found", "that this").
Refers back to prior proposition or thing ("it is absurd", "what it commands").
Preposition in multiple contexts ("in this case", "as for this case").
Indefinite article before nouns throughout ("a marked distinction", "a single class").
Used as the copular auxiliary in constructions like "to be" and "is".
Coordinates clauses/noun phrases frequently ("true and good", "difficult and complex").
Relativizer introducing clauses ("which of these", "which are").
First-person plural subject in direct propositions ("we leave", "we should").
Core ethical predicate appears as substantive notion ("good is good", "what is good"), not merely attributive adjective.
Links reasoning clauses in this text ("as above stated", "as the sense") and also prepositional uses; default to subordinator reading.
Demonstrative before noun or clause-bound reference ("this must not", "this and only this").
Negation particle throughout ("not another thing", "not open").
Copular auxiliary in multiword predicates ("things are", "they are").
Interrogative/relative form introducing unknown content ("what is real", "what exists").
Alternatives between coordinated elements ("that or that", "whether... or").
Preposition for means/agency/contrastive source phrases ("by itself", "by means of").
Auxiliary with perfect constructions ("have questions"; "have been").
First-person speaker pronoun in explicit self-reference ("I am", "if I am").
Contrasts claims or exceptions ("or ... but").
Modal auxiliary introducing possibility/permission ("may be", "may be observed").
Used as preposition meaning purpose/with respect to in contexts like "for knowledge", "for the same reason".
Introduces conditional clauses throughout ("if we consider", "if it was").
Comitative/association preposition in all inspected examples ("with this case", "with regard to").
3rd person plural pronoun referring to prior groups/claims ("they hold", "they may be").
Restrictive adverb in comparative and scope-sensitive contexts ("only this", "only possible").
Core lexical noun in ethics argument ("pleasure alone", "consciousness of pleasure").
Frequently the ethical noun in comparative and attributional constructions ("value of", "great value").
Indefinite article before noun (
, "an object").
Indefinite quantifier before nouns and as fallback object quantifier ("any reason", "any such").
Auxiliary in perfect-style predicates ("has followed", "has not").
Modal auxiliary with bare infinitive complements ("can shew", "can only").
Quantifier modifying noun groups ("all true", "all such"); used as determiner-like adjective in this corpus.
Numeric determiner in explicit comparisons and enumeration ("one kind", "one of them").
Future/modal auxiliary and in predictive constructions ("will prove", "will seem").
3rd person singular masculine pronoun ("he was", "he will maintain").
Scalar/degree adverb in ethical contrast and inferential clauses ("so urgent", "so that").
Contrastive determiner before noun or noun-like category ("other kinds", "other things").
Source/origin preposition ("from fact", "from this").
Negating noun phrases and determiners in constructions like "no objection", "no ground".
Modal auxiliary marking conditional or hypothetical futures.
Possessive determiner before noun ("its effects", "its realization").
Demonstrative-like determiner quantifying classes ("such as", "such judgments").
Modal auxiliary in necessity/falsity arguments ("must be", "must be shewn").
Core noun in phrases "good as a means" and "by means of".
Nominal item referenced repeatedly ("one thing", "this thing").
Noun in heading-like ethical inquiries throughout ("question of this").
Philosophical substance noun in organic-whole discussions.
Plural noun with explicit object readings ("all things", "other things").
Discipline name used as common noun for subject-matter.
Spatial/temporal/surface relation marker ("on this point", "on the course").
Plural demonstrative determiner before noun phrases.
Comparative/contrastive marker in comparative constructions.
Auxiliary in negative/interrogative and support constructions, e.g., "do there" claims.
Temporal/locative preposition in passages like "at that time".
Comparative/degree modifier across evaluative statements ("more desirable", "more probable").
Property adjective of propositions in epistemic discussion.
Standard noun for referent in ethical cognition/valuation contexts.
Indefinite determiner for quantities/classes ("some things", "some people").
1st-person possessive determiner in references to shared inquiry and conclusions.
Reflexive pronoun with self-reference ("itself as a whole").
Modal auxiliary in normative hypotheticals ("should be", "should be fulfilled").
Quantifying adjective introducing subsets ("certain kind", "certain relation").
Noun for propositional content and assertions.
Auxiliary in inversion/negation and support constructions ("does not").
Existential/locative placeholder in constructions like "is there" and "there may be".
Part of perfect forms and passive-like predicates.
Attributive modifier of questions/practices related to ethics.
Temporal/resultive adverb ("then" in argument sequencing).
Adjectival in evaluations of epistemic or practical attainability.
Clause-level temporal-subordinator ("when we say", "when this").
Additive modifier used to include additional points.
3rd person possessive determiner before noun ("his own happiness").
Object/oblique pronoun in first-person plural.
Scope adverb in emphasis and concession contexts.
Cardinal numeral introducing pairings and dual distinctions.
Finite mental-state verb in evaluative predicates ("think that", "what do you think").
Third-person plural object pronoun in many imperative and contrastive references.
Superlative-like degree word in noun phrases ("the most important").
Past-form auxiliary/copular with noun complements in multiple quotations.
Ethical-philosophical noun for conduct/behavior types.
Anatomical and metaphysical noun sense in part-whole arguments.
Plural demonstrative determiner ("those actions", "those theories").
Lexical verb in definitional phrasing ("what we mean").
Qualifying adjective indicating identity ("the same thing").
Intensifier before adjectives/adj-like clauses.
Target noun in discussions of ends and final causes.
Negative modal+auxiliary in argumentative constraints.
Degree adverb with comparative constructions ("far greater").
Central noun for ontological status and ethical predication.
Abstract noun for argumentative motivation.
Noun denoting mode or meaning in epistemic contexts.
Impersonal/evidential auxiliary introducing appearance judgments ("it seems").
Speech verb in explicit reporting and ascribing position.
3rd-person plural possessive determiner before noun phrases.
1st-person singular possessive determiner before noun.
Possessive/clitic marker in author-name forms (e.g., Mill’s), appearing without lexical content of its own.
Logical discourse connective/continuation marker linking conclusions.
Evaluative adjective for value properties.
Plural noun for behavioral items under ethical evaluation.
Temporal/frequency adverb in hypothetical universal claims.
Bare verb meaning to be present or occur in explanatory contexts.
Gradable adjective in comparative value claims.
Sequence/ordering adjective in numbered-stage argument structure.
Temporal adverb and discourse marker in method transitions.
Conjunctive quantifier with noun phrases ("both factors").
Demonstrative determiner for additional/alternate items.
Adverb of frequency for shared practical inference claims.
Attributive contrastive adjective for classes/types.
Verb in phrases like "regard as" and "with regard to".
Comparative adjective in optimization and value ranking contexts.
Indefinite pronoun for unspecified item/phenomenon.
Attributive aesthetic predicate in noun phrases.
Modal predicate adjective in logical and causal necessity contexts.
Ethical noun for undesirable states; not merely adjectival modifier in these contexts.
Common noun for kind or class being discussed.
Introduces indirect yes/no style alternatives in propositions.
